item.title: Dukkha as Ambivalence in Relational Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores dukkha through the lens of ambivalence in love, friendship, family, and group life. He moves from comic and psychoanalytic references to a practical Buddhist account of equanimity: how to stay with mixed feeling, resist the urge to control, and distinguish care from clinging. The talk closes by returning to compassion as love in the face of helplessness, and to the difficulty of living with the imperfection of others without hardening or taking over.
Chapters: 00:00 A Comic Opening - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=0 02:31 Love and Lack - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=151 05:54 Pain and Disconnection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=354 09:29 What Ambivalence Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=569 14:30 Freud and Mixed Feeling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=870 17:51 Equanimity Widens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=1071 24:57 Acceptance in Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=1497 29:01 Relationship and Dependence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=1741 39:44 Compassion Without Control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=2384
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/91355/

item.title: Talk: Knowing, Not-Knowing, Selfing
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores Dharma as a disciplined movement between knowing and not knowing: learning enough to see suffering clearly, while loosening the need to control experience with certainty. He connects self-knowledge to dialogue, sangha, and the willingness to let familiar identity become less solid. The talk gradually shifts from ideas about practice into a close examination of how selfing is built from habit, feeling tone, and subtle assumptions.
Chapters: 00:00 Knowing and Not Knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=0 02:30 Safety and Power - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=150 06:15 Dialogue and Inquiry - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=375 08:44 Narrative Fractures - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=524 11:43 Ignorance and Dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=703 17:39 Learning What Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=1059 24:14 Renunciation of Control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=1454 28:03 The Two Selves - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=1683 36:36 Listening Through Identity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22626/?t=2196
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22626

item.title: Evening Reflections - A Poem
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reads and reflects on a poem about impermanence, using a mountain retreat in New Mexico as the setting for a quiet confrontation with time, fragility, and change. The talk moves from the feel of austere landscape to a direct meditation on mortality, then toward a more spacious relationship to fear and grief. It ends with a simple emphasis on remembering, letting go, and allowing the warmth that can come with loss.
Chapters: 00:00 Mountain Austerity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22622/?t=0 00:50 A Poem Appears - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22622/?t=50 01:34 Stability Crumbles - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22622/?t=94 02:20 Fear Wakes Up - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22622/?t=140 02:59 Aliveness Opens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22622/?t=179 03:36 Remembering and Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22622/?t=216
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22622

item.title: Metta for Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a talk and guided practice on metta for oneself, beginning with a broad framing of love as something that makes human life bearable. He distinguishes the four Brahma-viharas as different species of love, then spends much of the talk showing how self-mettta can work as cultivation, concentration, and a way of meeting whatever pain arises. The practice settles into repeated phrases of blessing and a steady invitation to see oneself through kind, merciful eyes, ending with a sense of love as vast and measureless.
Chapters: 00:34 Love and human life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=34 01:51 Four kinds of love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=111 04:00 How metta works - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=240 06:29 What metta reveals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=389 09:25 Finding your own path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=565 12:28 A merciful gaze - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=748 18:10 Blessing the self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1090 24:52 Problems dissolving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1492 29:14 What has not been loved - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1754
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86532/

item.title: Moral Elevation, Moral Disgust and the Vastness Required to Open to Dukkha
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can sensitize us to goodness without losing contact with suffering. He moves from moral elevation and the force of ordinary courage into moral disgust, asking how care can stay clear of hatred, cynicism, and political reactivity. The talk gradually widens into a reflection on vastness, mortality, and impermanence, ending with Ajahn Chah’s tea cup as a way to hold life with gratitude and less fear.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1 01:02 Wesley Autry's Courage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=62 04:05 Sensitivity to Goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=245 06:53 Moral Disgust - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=413 14:13 Prophetic Speech - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=853 19:22 Distance from Rumination - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1162 24:25 Widening the Horizon - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1465 30:18 Already Broken - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1818
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21112
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can sensitize us to goodness without losing contact with suffering. He moves from moral elevation and the force of ordinary courage into moral disgust, asking how care can stay clear of hatred, cynicism, and political reactivity. The talk gradually widens into a reflection on vastness, mortality, and impermanence, ending with Ajahn Chah’s tea cup as a way to hold life with gratitude and less fear.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1 01:02 Wesley Autry's Courage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=62 04:05 Sensitivity to Goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=245 06:53 Moral Disgust - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=413 14:13 Prophetic Speech - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=853 19:22 Distance from Rumination - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1162 24:25 Widening the Horizon - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1465 30:18 Already Broken - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21112/?t=1818
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21112

item.title: Guided Meditation: Landing in the Breath and Soundscape
item.description:
(Big Bear Retreat Center) These recordings are available publicly from a retreat held in person at Big Bear Retreat Center. Learn more about the center and upcoming offerings for retreats in nature, gathered in community. bigbearretreatcenter.org/upcoming-retreats/ Healing the Self, Loving the Self, Forgetting the Self Matthew Brensilver July 9 - 13, 2024 Co-sponsored by Insight Retreat Center (IRC) and Big Bear Retreat Center In this silent retreat, we explore how mindfulness supports the healing of the self. Many of the Buddhist teachings help us to tend to painful memory, old pains and the habits that compound suffering. As we become more gentle and loving towards experience, the self becomes less and less of a preoccupation. The more completely we accept ourselves, the easier it becomes to forget the self and rest in an awareness unencumbered by self-consciousness. This retreat includes sitting and walking meditation instructions, and dharma talks.
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/85473/

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on words from Ajahn Sucitto
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short teaching from Ajahn Sucitto and uses it to unfold a quiet map of the path: generosity, ethics, renunciation, discernment, persistence, and patience. He moves from the felt meaning of sharing to the practical question of how to direct energy, make choices, and time action well. The talk stays grounded in ordinary examples and in the sense that practice is less about intensity than about steady, responsive care.
Chapters: 00:20 Reading the quote - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=20 02:39 Generosity as interdependence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=159 04:01 Sharing Dharma wisely - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=241 06:58 Ethics and kindness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=418 09:15 Morality and renunciation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=555 12:01 Steady persistence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=721 14:06 Patience and timing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=846 15:55 Closing reflections - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20531/?t=955
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20531

item.title: Metta Practice
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver presents metta as something more than a pleasant mood: a capacity that makes insight practice bearable, especially when the heart meets the full intensity of suffering. He moves through the four brahmaviharas as four distinct kinds of love, then explains how metta can cultivate warmth, steady the mind, or bring buried grief and fear to the surface for healing. The talk closes with a simple practice of repeating phrases, listening for their effect, and widening the field of care to others without denying war, oppression, or loss.
Chapters: 00:00 Love and Insight - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=0 02:28 Four Species of Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=148 06:33 Why Love Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=393 10:56 Meeting What Hurts - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=656 12:45 Settling the Body - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=765 16:55 Remembering Goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=1015 25:32 Abiding in Kindness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=1532 31:33 Extending the Wish - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=1893 37:04 A Sublime Abiding - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80506/?t=2224
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/80506/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Listening to the Mind of Buddha
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that gradually shifts from settling into the room toward a practice of listening: first to breath, body, sound, and the darkness behind closed eyes, then to a deeper receptivity he links to Leonard Cohen’s hummingbird lines. The talk emphasizes trusting awareness, letting silence beckon, and resting without over-managing experience. It closes by naming practice as a way to stop endlessly coping and simply be awake.
Chapters: 00:11 Arriving and Settling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=11 01:30 Silence Beckons - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=90 02:58 Simple Objects of Attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=178 05:04 Listening to Cohen - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=304 06:23 Trusting Awareness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=383 15:23 Forgiveness and Rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=923 17:33 Patience and Urgency - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=1053 23:35 Beyond Coping - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18176/?t=1415
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18176

item.title: Ill-Will & Metta
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores ill-will and metta as two very different ways the heart meets suffering. He moves from the mechanics of aversion—how craving, blame, and anger narrow experience—to the possibility of purification, radical accountability, and a softer, more spacious response. Near the end, he turns toward loving-kindness as a form of non-violence that can hold pain without collapse, and he closes by linking wisdom with love and non-contention.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the problem - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1 00:58 The Dharma as a broad remedy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=58 05:18 Purification and practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=318 08:07 What and how - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=487 12:37 Craving shapes the view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=757 17:40 Mara and the turning inward - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1060 23:41 The heat of aversion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1421 31:53 Facing and owning anger - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1913 38:30 Metta as antidote - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=2310
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17159

item.title: Guided Meditation - Grief and Compassion (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on grief that treats sorrow as a natural part of loving in an impermanent world. The practice moves from settling the body into phrases of tenderness, mercy, and reverence, with brief references to Leonard Cohen, anicca, and Stephen Levine to steady the listener in uncertainty. The tone stays intimate and practical, emphasizing that compassion is not an extra but a necessity when loss keeps arriving.
Chapters: 00:20 Settling In - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=20 01:27 Welcoming the Body - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=87 02:37 Love and Grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=157 05:40 Impermanence and Tenderness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=340 11:10 Traveling in Fog - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=670 15:31 Holding Sorrow - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=931 19:10 Love Deepened - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=1150 20:04 Tender Phrases - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70749/?t=1204
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/70749/

item.title: Wednesday Evening Reflection: Rohini's Enlightenment Poem
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a line from Rohini’s enlightenment poem: the path is not a line on a map, but a great shining world, enter wherever you like. He uses that image to gently undo the pressure to fit Dharma practice into a fixed shape, suggesting instead that we discover our own belonging within it. The talk closes with an encouragement to affirm one’s efforts, rest well, and continue noticing how apparent walls may become doorways.
Chapters: 03:26 A shining world - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=206 03:54 Not fitting in - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=234 04:36 Enter anywhere - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=276 05:19 Belonging and home - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=319 06:05 Nothing left behind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=365 06:47 From every direction - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=407 07:25 Affirming effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=445 08:05 Rest and continue - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=485
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12548

item.title: Death and the Poignancy of Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on death not as a grim abstraction but as a way to clarify what matters, soften the heart, and loosen the pressure to build a story around the self. He moves through examples from literature, Buddhist teachings, psychology, and personal memory to show how mortality can either harden us into fear or open us toward grief, love, and responsibility. The talk ends by returning to practice as a kind of training in letting go and in making friendship with the mind.
Chapters: 00:23 Opening the topic - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=23 03:07 Writing and dancing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=187 10:03 Birth and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=603 16:55 Practicing with death - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1015 25:30 Resisting and meaning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1530 29:40 Finitude and grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1780 36:54 Loss makes life vivid - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=2214 41:27 Fear, identity, and softness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=2487 50:53 Becoming and letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=3053
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60274/

item.title: Reflecting On An Ajahn Sucitto Quote
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on an Ajahn Sucitto teaching about why stopping feels difficult: the self stays busy trying to find ground, while practice asks for a deeper loving acceptance of experience as it is. He moves from busyness and silence to self-comparison, mindfulness, and the way a softer relationship to self can make room for more freedom and less fear. The talk closes with philosophical references and a quiet devotional ending that turns the discussion toward welfare, holding, and non-clinging.
Chapters: 00:34 Ajahn Sucitto's quote - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=34 04:09 Busyness as human condition - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=249 09:10 Solitude and avoidance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=550 12:26 Self, agitation, and comparison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=746 20:16 Mind and narrative self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1216 25:19 Loving the mess - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1519 33:01 Non-dwelling and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1981 40:25 Closing blessing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=2425
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9909

item.title: guided meditation
item.description:
(New York Insight Meditation Center) Meditation practice cultivates a diverse set of attentional, emotional and introspective skills. Central to the practice of mindfulness is the stabilization of attention. But before our attention stabilizes, practice can be overstimulating. This talk will explore the process through which the mind comes to rest. In developing this steadiness, equanimity (the capacity to fully permit the flow of both pleasure and pain) is a vital skill. We will see how concentration and equanimity reinforce each other and support a deeper understanding of ourselves. And how this stability, in turn, makes space for the heart to respond with joy and compassion.
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/55368/
